text:
Check out Sports Illustrated芒聙聶s Daily Rings, our daily Olympics podcast from Mitch Goldich and Dan Gartland. Subscribe wherever you get your podcasts (Apple,Spotify) and find clips onSI芒聙聶s YouTube page.

In three appearances at the French Open, Qinwen Zheng of China announced herself and gave an amuse-bouche of her considerable skills, once reaching the fourth round. But on Saturday, she fully announced herself. Under gorgeous conditions, above the clay at Roland Garros, the 21-year-old scored the biggest win of her ascending career, taking out the Croatian veteran Donna Vekic 6芒聙聯2,6芒聙聯3, to take the women芒聙聶s singles gold-medal match at the Paris Olympics.脗

Mixing her easy power with real grace on clay, Zheng played with true authority and was the better player in virtually every dimension. This was the classic case of meeting the moment. Coming as she does from a country that puts a great premium on Olympic success芒聙聰and the symbolic show of national exceptionalism that comes with it芒聙聰Zheng portrayed poise to match her tennis skills.

The triumph also spoke to Zheng芒聙聶s ability to reset. In her first match芒聙聰a bit of foreshadowing芒聙聰she turned in a double bagel in a 6芒聙聯0, 6芒聙聯0 defeat against Italy芒聙聶s Sara Errani. From there, her campaign got increasingly more difficult. In the third round, she defeated Emma Navarro of the U.S. and then deftly deflected Navarro芒聙聶svague complaintsthat Zheng was not a competitor worthy of respect. (芒聙聹I will not consider it an attack because she lost the match,芒聙聺 Zheng replied. A perfect, if inadvertent, response. ) Zheng then regrouped and beat Germany芒聙聶s Angelique Kerber 7芒聙聯6 in the third set, thereby endingKerber芒聙聶s Hall of Fame career.

In the semifinals, she scored the upset of the tournament, if not of the year. Playing Poland芒聙聶s Iga Swiatek at Roland Garros is a proposition akin to swimming against Michael Phelps. Yet Zhang took out the overwhelming favorite for gold, matching the four-time French Open champion shot for shot, and simply playing better when it mattered most.脗

Emboldened, not spent, from that signature win, Zheng saved her best for last.Everything about her performance on Saturday conveyed the message:I beat Swiatek in her house. There芒聙聶s no way I芒聙聶m not going to finish the job.

A word about Vekic: At every other WTA tournament the losing player is deemed afinalist.Here, they are calledsilver medalists.Especially for a player so fiercely patriotic芒聙聰who stayed in the village; who spent free moments watching other Croatian athletes compete芒聙聰Vekic will shake off the disappointment and take great satisfaction in her Olympic campaign.脗

In the third round, she scored one of the biggest wins of her career, taking out Coco Gauff, the young American who was not only seeded second but was Team USA芒聙聶s flag bearer along with LeBron James. Vekic followed that up with a quarterfinal win over Marta Kostyuk芒聙聰a sentimental favorite given her Ukrainian provenance芒聙聰winning 10芒聙聯8 in the match tiebreak, just desserts for the Wimbledon defeat. In the semifinals, no doubt motivated by the fact that overwhelming Swiatek had been eliminated, Vekic met the moment and simply outclassed her Slovakian opponent, Anna Karol脙颅na Schmiedlov脙隆. In the final, it was her turn to be outclassed. But she leaves with her head high and will look to continue her best year as a pro later this month at the U.S. Open.

Same, of course, for Zheng. A finalist at the Australian Open in January, she pierced the top 10 and became the most accomplished Chinese player since Li Na.脗

Zheng received no additional ranking points for this week芒聙聶s triumph, but will likely be a force not only at the U.S. Open, but in the general tennis firmament over the next decade. She received no direct prize money from the Olympics芒聙聰but her star wattage and off-court earning potential will likely make her a millionaire 10 times over.

Here芒聙聶s what芒聙聶s undeniable: Zheng will forever be known as an Olympic gold medal winner.

Jon Wertheim is a senior writer for Sports Illustrated and has been part of the full-time SI writing staff since 1997, largely focusing on the tennis beat, sports business and social issues, and enterprise journalism. In addition to his work at SI, he is a correspondent for 芒聙聹60 Minutes芒聙聺 and a commentator for The Tennis Channel. He has authored 11 books and has been honored with two Emmys, numerous writing and investigative journalism awards, and the Eugene Scott Award from the International Tennis Hall of Fame. Wertheim is a longtime member of the New York Bar Association (retired), the International Tennis Writers Association and the Writers Guild of America. He has a bachelor芒聙聶s in history from Yale University and received a law degree from the University of Pennsylvania. He resides in New York City and Paris with his wife, who is a divorce mediator and adjunct law professor. They have two children.

title: Qinwen Zheng Meets the Moment at the Paris Olympics
description: The tennis star won a gold medal for China, defeating Donna Vekic of Croatia in straight sets at Roland Garros.
